name: Resilience Policy Framing
consequences: Pulling this lever shifts the narrative from platform dependence to national resilience, making procurement committees more receptive to supplier-neutral clauses. It also raises the bar for evidence, requiring detailed risk assessments that may delay initial approvals. The framing may attract scrutiny from incumbent vendors who could lobby for broader definitions of resilience that dilute platform neutrality.
options: Position platform neutrality as a core resilience requirement in all future digital identity procurement documents.
Reframe the dependency issue as a continuity‑of‑service risk that mandates fallback authentication pathways.
Highlight the geopolitical exposure of relying on foreign OS vendors to justify mandatory platform‑neutral clauses.
review: Controls narrative framing vs. technical feasibility. Weakness: The options overlook the need for concrete technical evidence to support policy claims.

name: Stakeholder Coalition Architecture
consequences: Building a diverse coalition increases political leverage but also introduces coordination overhead and potential conflicting agendas. A broad coalition can pressure Digitaliseringsstyrelsen to adopt fallback requirements, yet it may dilute focus if too many stakeholders push for unrelated reforms. The coalition’s credibility hinges on sustained engagement, which requires dedicated resources.
options: Form a cross‑sector alliance of civil society, academia, and security experts to co‑author the feasibility report.
Engage key parliamentary members early to secure formal endorsements of the platform‑neutral agenda.
Create a working group of private sector vendors that can provide alternative platform solutions.
review: Controls coalition breadth vs. focused action. Weakness: The options do not address potential conflicts of interest among coalition members.

name: Demonstrator Development Focus
consequences: Prioritizing mobile‑oriented demonstrators validates the technical feasibility of fallback authentication on privacy‑respecting OSes, but may divert resources from browser‑based solutions. A lean demonstrator scope keeps the project within budget but risks missing critical integration points with existing identity providers. Demonstrators that are too narrow may fail to convince procurement bodies of broader applicability.
options: Build a FIDO2/WebAuthn demonstrator on GrapheneOS to prove mobile fallback viability.
Develop a browser‑based fallback using WebAuthn and OpenID Connect to showcase platform neutrality.
Prototype a hardware‑token fallback that bypasses mobile OS entirely.
review: Controls demonstrator scope vs. stakeholder relevance. Weakness: The options ignore the need for interoperability testing with live MitID environments.

name: Procurement Language Integration
consequences: Embedding platform‑neutral clauses in procurement documents can lock in future suppliers but may trigger legal challenges from incumbent vendors. It also raises the technical specification bar, potentially excluding smaller vendors who cannot meet stringent fallback requirements. Successful integration can set a precedent for other national identity projects.
options: Draft a procurement addendum that mandates platform‑neutral authentication pathways.
Propose a mandatory technical specification for fallback authentication in AltID tender documents.
Introduce a compliance checklist that verifies absence of platform lock‑in before award.
review: Controls procurement rigidity vs. innovation. Weakness: The options do not consider the impact on vendor selection timelines.

name: EU Standards Alignment
consequences: Aligning Danish proposals with EU Digital Identity Wallet standards amplifies influence but requires navigating complex EU policy cycles. It may delay national procurement if EU requirements lag behind domestic needs. Successful alignment can attract EU funding and broader acceptance of fallback mechanisms.
options: Submit technical contributions to the EU Digital Identity Wallet working group on platform neutrality.
Align the Danish fallback concept with the EU's eIDAS2 interoperability framework.
Advocate for a European directive mandating fallback authentication in digital identity systems.
review: Controls EU alignment vs. national urgency. Weakness: The options overlook the time lag inherent in EU legislative processes.

name: Institutional Risk Management
consequences: Implementing robust risk management reduces exposure to supplier concentration but adds administrative overhead. It may also create a perception of excessive bureaucracy that slows decision‑making. Proper risk frameworks can secure stakeholder confidence and justify additional funding.
options: Establish a risk register that tracks platform dependency and fallback readiness.
Conduct a supplier concentration audit to quantify exposure to Apple and Google.
Develop a contingency plan that outlines fallback activation procedures during platform outages.
review: Controls risk documentation vs. operational agility. Weakness: The options fail to address how to integrate risk findings into procurement decisions.

name: Platform Integrity Signal Diversification
consequences: Pulling this lever expands the set of device integrity signals beyond Apple and Google, reducing the single point of failure in the trust model. It introduces additional verification steps for identity providers and may increase the certification burden for app developers. The broader signal set also enables more granular risk assessment of device trustworthiness.
options: Integrate GrapheneOS attestation into the MitID verification flow to provide an open-source integrity signal.
Leverage hardware-backed attestation via TPM modules in enterprise devices to offer a platform-neutral trust anchor.
Develop a community-driven attestation service that aggregates device integrity metrics from multiple open-source platforms.
review: Core tension: dependence on proprietary platform integrity signals versus the need for a diversified trust model. Weakness: The options overlook the potential certification delays and developer adoption hurdles.

name: Fallback Authentication Architecture
consequences: Pulling this lever establishes a secondary authentication path that does not rely on mobile OS duopoly, ensuring continuity of service if platform policies change. It requires users to adopt new authentication methods and may increase onboarding friction. The fallback path also demands coordination with hardware vendors and standards bodies to guarantee interoperability.
options: Implement a browser-based WebAuthn fallback that uses secure enclave tokens for device-agnostic authentication.
Deploy USB-C hardware tokens preloaded with user credentials to provide a plug-and-play fallback.
Create a mobile app that authenticates via QR code scanned by an external device, decoupling from OS integrity signals.
review: Core tension: reliance on mobile OS integrity versus the need for a platform-neutral fallback. Weakness: The options do not address user experience trade-offs and potential security gaps in QR-based flows.

name: Digital Identity Supply Chain Decoupling
consequences: Pulling this lever reduces the supply chain concentration by enabling identity apps to be distributed through non-Apple/Google channels. It may trigger regulatory scrutiny over app vetting and could slow down deployment due to unfamiliar distribution ecosystems. The decoupling also opens opportunities for local developers to contribute to the identity stack.
options: Publish a MitID-compatible app on F-Droid with signed binaries and a transparent build process.
Establish a national app store for certified identity apps that enforces strict security reviews.
Encourage third-party app stores to host identity apps with a dedicated vetting pipeline for authentication services.
review: Core tension: centralized app distribution versus the need for a diversified supply chain. Weakness: The options ignore the potential legal barriers and user trust issues with alternative app stores.

name: Cross-Platform Trust Anchor Standardization
consequences: Pulling this lever creates a unified attestation schema that all operating systems can adopt, simplifying integration for identity providers. It requires consensus among OS vendors and may delay deployment if standards negotiations stall. The standardization also reduces fragmentation in the trust model, improving overall security posture.
options: Define a cross-platform attestation schema endorsed by EU digital identity working groups.
Mandate inclusion of an attestation API in both Android and iOS to support uniform verification.
Create a neutral attestation service that all OSes can query, decoupling from proprietary APIs.
review: Core tension: fragmented OS attestation mechanisms versus the need for a unified trust anchor. Weakness: The options do not consider the commercial interests of OS vendors and the cost of implementation.

name: Public Sector Digital Sovereignty Advocacy
consequences: Pulling this lever pushes policy changes that mandate platform neutrality in digital identity procurement, strengthening national sovereignty. It may face political resistance and slow legislative processes. The advocacy also raises public awareness, potentially influencing future procurement decisions.
options: Launch a public awareness campaign highlighting platform dependency risks to build citizen support.
Draft a legislative amendment requiring platform-neutral authentication in all public sector digital services.
Propose a national digital identity task force to oversee platform neutrality and coordinate stakeholder input.
review: Core tension: political inertia versus the need for digital sovereignty. Weakness: The options overlook the need for cross-sector collaboration and potential budget constraints.

name: Contingency Pathway Institutionalization
consequences: Pulling this lever embeds fallback procedures into institutional governance, ensuring rapid response to platform disruptions. It requires establishing new committees and formal protocols, which may increase administrative overhead. The institutionalization also provides a clear escalation path for continuity of service.
options: Establish a dedicated contingency committee within Digitaliseringsstyrelsen to oversee fallback readiness.
Create a formal fallback protocol with defined escalation steps and responsibilities.
Implement a simulation exercise to test fallback readiness across multiple public sector services.
review: Core tension: lack of formal contingency planning versus the need for institutionalized fallback mechanisms. Weakness: The options do not address resource allocation and training requirements for the new structures.

name: Alternative Trust Anchor Integration
consequences: Pulling this lever would shift the identity verification chain to include a set of open-source, community-maintained cryptographic libraries as primary trust anchors, reducing reliance on proprietary platform APIs. This change would increase audit transparency but also require additional developer effort to maintain the libraries and ensure compatibility with existing eIDAS compliance checks. It may also expose the system to new attack vectors if the libraries are not rigorously vetted.
options: Deploy a federated network of independent, verifiable hardware security modules that serve as distributed trust anchors for authentication events.
Integrate a blockchain-based attestation layer that records device integrity proofs in a tamper-evident ledger accessible to all identity providers.
Adopt a consortium of academic research labs to host a shared, open-source trust anchor service that undergoes regular third-party security audits.
review: Core tension between proprietary platform trust anchors and open-source alternatives. Weakness: The options overlook the operational overhead of maintaining distributed trust anchor infrastructure.

name: Dynamic Procurement Clause Modeling
consequences: Pulling this lever would embed adaptive procurement clauses that automatically trigger fallback authentication requirements when vendor market concentration exceeds a threshold. This would compel future contracts to include platform-neutral fallback provisions, but could also slow procurement cycles due to increased contractual complexity. It may also create ambiguity in vendor selection criteria, leading to disputes over compliance interpretation.
options: Introduce a market concentration index threshold that, when surpassed, mandates inclusion of fallback authentication clauses in all new procurement documents.
Require vendors to provide a documented fallback authentication pathway as part of their bid, with penalties for non-compliance.
Implement a rotating vendor evaluation schedule that forces periodic reassessment of platform dependency risks and adjusts procurement language accordingly.
review: Tension between speed of procurement and resilience through fallback clauses. Weakness: The options fail to address how to enforce compliance once clauses are included.

name: Cross-Platform Credential Portability Initiative
consequences: Pulling this lever would standardize credential formats across operating systems, enabling users to transfer authentication tokens between platforms without loss of security guarantees. This would reduce platform lock-in but could increase the attack surface if token migration processes are not tightly controlled. It may also require significant coordination with OS vendors and standards bodies.
options: Develop a universal credential serialization format that is OS-agnostic and signed by a neutral authority.
Create a middleware service that translates platform-specific tokens into a common format on the fly during authentication.
Establish a certification program for third-party apps that can securely handle cross-platform credential migration.
review: Core tension between platform-specific credential handling and user mobility. Weakness: The options do not consider the regulatory approval needed for cross-platform token handling.

name: Hardware Token Backchannel Enforcement
consequences: Pulling this lever would mandate that critical authentication flows include a hardware token backchannel that can be used when mobile OS integrity signals are unavailable. This would enhance resilience but would increase user onboarding complexity and require secure distribution of tokens. It may also raise privacy concerns if token usage is monitored.
options: Require all public-sector services to support a YubiKey-based authentication fallback that can be activated via a secure web portal.
Implement a smartcard-based backchannel that can be issued to citizens through local government offices.
Deploy a mobile-OS independent NFC token that can be used as a secondary authentication factor during platform outages.
review: Tension between user convenience and resilience through hardware backchannels. Weakness: The options ignore the logistical challenges of token distribution and lifecycle management.

name: Regulatory Sandbox for Platform-Neutral Identity
consequences: Pulling this lever would create a controlled regulatory sandbox where alternative identity solutions can be tested without full compliance burdens. This would accelerate innovation but could delay broader adoption if sandbox results are not quickly translated into policy. It may also create a perception of unequal treatment among vendors.
options: Establish a temporary, low-risk certification pathway for platform-neutral identity prototypes that allows rapid field testing.
Offer tax incentives to developers who build fallback authentication modules within the sandbox environment.
Create a joint task force between regulators and academia to evaluate sandbox outcomes and recommend policy adjustments.
review: Tension between regulatory rigor and rapid experimentation. Weakness: The options do not address how sandbox findings will be integrated into formal procurement processes.

name: Public Awareness Campaign on Digital Sovereignty
consequences: Pulling this lever would educate citizens about the risks of platform dependency and the benefits of fallback authentication, potentially increasing public pressure on policymakers. This could accelerate policy change but may also polarize public opinion and create resistance to perceived government overreach. It may also divert resources from technical work.
options: Launch a nationwide media series that explains digital sovereignty concepts through relatable stories and interactive demos.
Partner with universities to host workshops that demonstrate fallback authentication in real-world scenarios.
Develop an online portal that allows citizens to test fallback authentication flows and report issues directly to policymakers.
review: Core tension between public engagement and technical focus. Weakness: The options overlook the need for sustained engagement beyond initial campaigns.
